beats.narration_text: Ahoj — this is Liam, in for Bear. Today: sim-scout, the skill that finds concepts where running the rule teaches what stating the rule cannot.

output: two testable predictions required per card

beats.narration_text: The skill is text-only: one SKILL dot md, two card sources, one output file. The heavy lift is applying the sim bar to each candidate and classifying it as MANIM — a directed animation you watch — or D3 slash DATAVIZ — an emergent process you run and poke.

props.title: WATCH it or POKE it.
files: SKILL.md — sim bar + lane rules + card schema

beats.narration_text: The pipeline: ensure the chapter manifest exists, harvest any simulate-with-Claude exercises first, read chapters for additional candidates, apply the three-part sim bar, classify each into MANIM or D3, fill all mandatory card fields — rule, artifact as motion, two predictions — then write to simulation-ideas dot md.

props.title: Sim bar → classify → card.

phases.label: CARD
phases.note: rule, artifact as motion, 2 predictions — stop here

props.footerNote: Append, never replace. Continue numbering from highest existing Candidate NN.

beats.narration_text: Self-demo: scout organic-chemistry Chapter five — Stereochemistry at Tetrahedral Centers.

props.runningText: applying sim bar to Stereochemistry chapter...
output: rule present, motion obvious — checking sim bar

beats.narration_text: A real MANIM lane card: animate two enantiomers of lactic acid rotating in 3D and failing to superimpose. The rule is fixed (tetrahedral carbon), the artifact moves (rotation with alignment attempt), and two predictions are checkable — swap any two substituents and superimposition succeeds, swap exactly two to flip R/S assignment. Score nine of ten.

props.title: CARD — organic-chemistry ch05 (MANIM lane)
props.code:
## Candidate 01 — Enantiomers: The Non-Superimposition
- Source: organic-chemistry/chapters/05-stereochemistry.md
- Lane: MANIM (directed animation)
- Rule: tetrahedral C with 4 different substituents →
  two non-superimposable mirror images
- Artifact: lactic acid rotation — two enantiomers
  approach, rotate, fail to align (Manim 3D)
- P1: if any two substituents match, overlap succeeds
- P2: swapping exactly two substituents flips R/S
- Human supplies: Nothing — fully synthetic
- Output medium: Manim (3D rotation mp4)
- Score: 9/10

beats.narration_text: Mechanism one: the sim earns its place when running the rule teaches what stating the rule cannot. A definition is a label. A simulation is a demonstration. The sim bar exists to tell them apart: simulatable means a visible artifact exists, verifiable means you can be wrong, surprising means there is a reason to watch.

props.heading: Three gates, not one.
props.body: Simulatable: a visible curve, trajectory, or distribution exists. Verifiable: at least two testable predictions — boundary cases, known limits, exact ratios. Surprising: the intuitive answer fails, or a parameter does something unexpected. All three are required. A sim that passes two is not a candidate yet.
props.quote: running the rule teaches what stating the rule cannot

beats.narration_text: Mechanism two: WATCH or POKE, one question. If the payoff is a scripted animation of a known rule playing out deterministically — a curve drawing, an orbit sweeping — it is MANIM. If the value is watching data update as a rule iterates, or letting the viewer drag a parameter and see the system respond — it is D3. A concept that genuinely wants both is two cards.

props.heading: WATCH or POKE.
props.body: MANIM: one directed, cinematic animation of a deterministic rule — physics laws, continuous fields, geometry morphs. D3: emergent, stochastic, or interactive — Monte-Carlo filling, agent dynamics, parameter sweeps. The lane determines the builder and the output format. When genuinely torn, write two cards.
props.quote: is the payoff a directed animation of a known rule (you WATCH it), or an emergent/interactive data process (you RUN and POKE it)?

beats.narration_text: Mechanism three: the verification gate. Two testable predictions are mandatory before a card is written. No predictions — not a card yet. This is the same gate that stops a scientist from presenting a model with no falsification criteria. A sim without two checkable predictions is a demonstration, not a test. Route it to the plain scout instead.

props.heading: Two predictions. Mandatory.
props.body: The builder uses the two predictions as the check beat: does the sim produce what it should at the boundaries? The scout that can't name two predictions hasn't finished thinking. A sim without a verification gate is indistinguishable from a pretty animation.
props.quote: If you can't name two testable predictions, it is not a sim card yet.

beats.narration_text: Sim-scout: the sim bar, the WATCH-or-POKE classification, and the two-predictions gate. The human picks. The simulation builder builds.

artifactLines: verification gate: 2 testable predictions or no card

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Run sim-scout on a chapter with an equation you've seen plotted statically. Can you state two predictions the sim must pass? Can you describe the artifact as motion — not as a frame? If not — the card needs more work.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Simulating. I'm Liam, in for Bear.
